articles.title: Quote of the Day by Legendary German philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche: “I'm not upset that you lied to me, I'm upset that... – Inspiring lessons on relationships, honesty, understanding and why trust is built in years, broken in seconds by the writer known for his deep insights into human nature
articles.description: Quote of the Day by Friedrich Nietzsche: Friedrich Nietzsche’s quote, “I'm not upset that you lied to me, I'm upset that from now on I can't believe you,” explores the deep emotional impact of broken trust in human relationships. The quote suggests that the real pain of dishonesty is not the single act of lying, but the long-term damage it causes to faith, confidence, and emotional security between people.

articles.title: Greek Proverb of the Day: “A woman who cooks with a knife doesn’t joke” — Life lessons on strength, control, power, confidence, and why underestimating people can be your biggest mistake
articles.description: Greek Proverb of the Day highlights the meaning of “A woman who cooks with a knife doesn’t joke.” The saying reflects the idea that a woman who understands her strength, responsibilities, and control over her environment should not be underestimated or provoked. It suggests that true power is often expressed through confidence, skill, discipline, and calm authority rather than loud behavior or open display.

articles.title: Quote of the day by American philosopher Judith Butler: ‘The violence of language consists in its effort to capture the...’ – ‘Gender Trouble’ author on why words can never fully contain truth
articles.description: Judith Butler argues that language's attempt to fully define ineffable experiences, like love or grief, can be violent, destroying their essence. This quote highlights how words can limit reality, and Butler's work emphasizes that identity is fluid and shaped by social forces, not fixed definitions. Language should remain dynamic, allowing for ambiguity and growth.

articles.title: Breaking News: West Bengal Launches Detention Plan Amid Crackdown on Alleged Illegal Immigrants
articles.description: Political developments in West Bengal have intensified following the launch of a new crackdown initiative targeting alleged illegal immigrants. The ruling Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) government, led by Chief Minister Suvendu Adhikari as per the claims in circulation, has begun implementing its electoral promise of identifying and acting against individuals residing illegally in the state. As part of this initiative, “holding centres” have been set up across multiple districts, which function similarly to detention facilities. These centres are intended to temporarily house suspected illegal immigrants while verification and legal procedures are completed. Authorities claim the move is aimed at strengthening border security and addressing long-standing concerns over infiltration. Reports indicate that several individuals, including suspected Bangladeshi nationals, have already been shifted to these holding centres in districts such as Malda and Murshidabad. In one such facility located in Malda’s English Bazar area, multiple individuals, including women and minors, are reportedly being held under surveillance. Security arrangements at these centres include CCTV monitoring along with deployment of police personnel and civil defence volunteers. Officials state that individuals identified as foreign nationals without valid documentation will be processed under government guidelines, which include temporary detention for up to 30 days while their identity and legal status are verified. Those found to be residing illegally may face deportation proceedings. The political discourse around the issue has become increasingly sharp, with strong statements made during public rallies and campaign events emphasizing strict action against infiltration. Authorities have also highlighted the importance of maintaining law and order while ensuring due legal process is followed.

articles.title: Exclusion From Voter List Doesn’t End Citizenship: Supreme Court Top Quotes On SIR
articles.description:
The Supreme Court of India on Wednesday upheld the Election Commission’s Special Intensive Revision (SIR) process of electoral rolls, saying the exercise was within the constitutional powers of the poll body and could not be termed unconstitutional.

A bench comprising Chief Justice Surya Kant and Justice Joymalya Bagchi delivered the verdict after hearing multiple petitions challenging the legality of the SIR process.

Here are the top observations made by the Supreme Court:

“Free And Fair Elections Are Not Just About Polling”

The court observed that democracy does not depend only on the process of voting but also on maintaining accurate electoral rolls.

“Free and fair elections do not rest merely upon mechanics of polling,” the bench said while defending the Election Commission’s authority to revise voter lists.

Also Read: Supreme Court Upholds Bihar SIR, Says Election Commission Acted Within Its Powers

“Election Commission Acted Within Its Powers”

The Supreme Court ruled that the Election Commission had exercised powers granted to it under the Constitution and the Representation of the People Act.

“The Election Commission has exercised its powers, not acted beyond them,” the court observed, adding that the entire SIR process cannot be labelled unconstitutional.

Also Read: Siddaramaiah To Get Rajya Sabha Seat? Speculations Rife Amid Karnataka Power Change Buzz

“Removal From Voter List Does Not Mean Citizenship Ends”

One of the court’s key remarks was that exclusion from the electoral roll should not be interpreted as loss of citizenship.

The bench clarified that the Election Commission is not empowered to determine citizenship status. However, it said suspicious cases can be referred to the Central government for further examination.

“SIR Was Conducted As Per Need”

The court said the steps taken during the Special Intensive Revision were necessary and proportionate considering the scale of the exercise.

It also rejected arguments that the burden of proof had been unfairly shifted entirely onto voters.

The bench noted that even if a person had shifted residence, they or their family could still have records in earlier voter lists, meaning the process was not arbitrary.

“Election Commission Can Reject Doubtful Documents”

The Supreme Court said the poll body was entitled to verify the credibility of documents submitted by voters.

“The Election Commission can refuse inclusion if documents do not appear genuine,” the court said, adding that such scrutiny cannot automatically be interpreted as a citizenship determination exercise.

Challenge Against SIR Process

The petitions had challenged the Election Commission’s SIR exercise, arguing that it went beyond powers granted under Article 326 of the Constitution and the Representation of the People Act, 1950.

Petitioners objected particularly to the requirement that voters missing from electoral rolls prepared in 2002 or 2003 in certain states must now establish proof of citizenship and ancestral linkage with persons whose names appeared in earlier voter lists.

However, the Supreme Court ultimately concluded that the SIR exercise satisfies constitutional and legal requirements and that the Election Commission has the authority to frame procedures for such a large-scale revision process.
